review_text: There is no villain here. Mia and Sebastian do not fail each other through betrayal or cruelty, they are simply shaped by the quiet demands of adulthood. Their love is genuine, but love alone is not enough.La La Land isn’t a tragedy or a fairy tale. It’s a reflection on timing, sacrifice, and the lives we build alongside the love we outgrow. You leave the film heartbroken, but contemplative, aware that somewhere, in another version of your life, a different song is still playing.

review_text: 100-word review: A colourful, dreamy throwback to old-school Hollywood musicals that’s easy to get swept up in, at least some of the time. Sometimes you can’t have both love and ambition, and chasing your dream might mean losing something else along the way — it's a message with staying power. The jazz storyline, while central, comes off as preachy however, with Gosling’s character reduced to a stubborn caricature; we get it Damien Chazelle, you like jazz... The song-and-dance sequences vary from good to serviceable, but don't really wow me overall. Then again, I'm just not much of a musical enthusiast to begin with.

review_text: Musicals aren’t even my thing but I had such a great time with this. The fact that the genre isn’t my cup of tea probably limits the ceiling for my rating, but regardless I can’t help but appreciate Chazelle’s style and direction. Throughout all the music, there’s a gripping storyline that’s driven by great on-screen chemistry between Ryan Gosling and Emma Stone. The ending was the perfect conclusion to such an emotional rollercoaster. I definitely plan on revisiting this at some point, maybe on a rewatch I’ll end up appreciating this even more and hopefully upgrade my rating.
